title: Second conditional
explanation:
Second conditional

If + past simple, would + verb

We use the second conditional for unreal or imaginary situations now or in the future.

  • If I had a million pounds, I would travel the world. (I don't have a million pounds.)
  • If I were you, I would accept the offer.

After I/he/she we often use were in formal English: If I were rich…

questions.text: If I [*won|win|would win] the lottery, I would buy a house.
If she [*were|was|is] here, she would help us.
I [*would travel|'d travelling|travel] more if I had more time.
If they [*knew|know|would know] the truth, they would be shocked.
What [*would|will|do] you do if you lost your job?
If I [*were|am|be] you, I would apologise.

exercise_sets.title: Exercise 2
exercise_sets.instructions: Choose the correct option for each gap.

questions.text: If he had a car, he ____ to work by car. | *would drive | will drive | drove
If I ____ more money, I would move to the coast. | *had | have | will have
She would be happier if she ____ less. | *worked | works | will work
If we ____ in Paris, we would visit the Louvre every week. | *lived | live | would live
If I [*spoke|speak|will speak] perfect English, I would feel more confident.
They [*wouldn't buy|won't buy|don't buy] that house if it were too expensive.
If it [*rained|rain|would rain] tomorrow, the match would be cancelled.
I would help you if I [*could|can|will can].
If she [*didn't live|doesn't live|won't live] so far away, we would meet more often.
